dialogue.text: You have a LoanApplication object with an applicant name, amount, term, interest type, guarantor, collateral, and several optional flags. The constructor is becoming unmanageable. How do you clean this up?

Builder with a fluent API. Each method sets one aspect of the application and returns 'this'. A final Build() validates and returns the application. Callers only chain the methods relevant to their case.
Could you not just use a C# object initializer instead?

For simple cases, yes, and I would prefer it. Builder earns its place when there are cross-field validation rules or computed defaults that an initializer cannot enforce. A loan without an applicant or a zero amount should never be creatable.
Where exactly do you enforce that a loan application must have an amount greater than zero?

In Build(). That is the single choke point. I throw before returning the finished object, so a LoanApplication in the wild is always valid. Spreading validation across individual setters means partial objects can escape.
How do you ensure the final LoanApplication is immutable?
Have Build() construct a record with init-only properties from the builder's accumulated state. The builder builds; the result is locked.
What happens if a developer calls Build() twice on the same builder instance?

If Build() returns a reference to the same internal mutable object, both callers alias the same instance. The fix is to either snapshot the state into a fresh object at Build() time, or reset internal state after each Build() call.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: When is a C# object initializer enough?
followUpsQA.a: When construction is just setting independent properties with no steps or cross-field validation.

followUpsQA.q: Where does .NET use the Builder pattern?
followUpsQA.a: WebApplication.CreateBuilder, HostBuilder, and HttpClient pipeline configuration.

followUpsQA.q: How do you produce an immutable result from a Builder?
followUpsQA.a: Have Build() construct a record with init-only properties from the accumulated mutable state.

followUpsQA.q: Why validate in Build() rather than each setter?
followUpsQA.a: Cross-field rules need the full picture. Build() is the single point where the object is complete.

questions.q: What is the Prototype pattern, and how does cloning relate to it in C#?

answer.oneLiner: Prototype creates new objects by cloning an existing configured instance rather than constructing from scratch, which is the right choice when copying a template is cheaper or simpler than rebuilding it.
keyPoints: Shallow copy shares reference members between the original and the clone. Mutating a List on the clone also mutates the original.
Records' 'with' expression gives a non-destructive shallow copy. It is still shallow for any reference-type field such as List or Dictionary.
Deep-clone mutable reference members explicitly: with { Sections = new List<string>(original.Sections) }.
Only reach for Prototype when construction is genuinely expensive or a pre-configured template is the natural starting point.
answer.detail: MemberwiseClone copies field values, not what the fields point to. The 'with' expression on records does the same. Deep copies require cloning nested objects manually or serialising and deserialising the graph.

code.note: 'with' copies field values but shares reference objects. Deep-clone collections explicitly to avoid shared mutable state.

answer.analogy: Always deep-clone what you intend to own independently.
redFlags: Assuming MemberwiseClone or records' 'with' produces a deep copy. Both are shallow.
Sharing mutable collections between original and clone, causing silent aliasing bugs.
Using Prototype where a plain constructor is cheap and clear. It adds complexity for no gain.

dialogue.text: Your legal platform has a master contract template with dozens of configured clauses. Users create client-specific variations of it constantly. How do you handle that efficiently?
Prototype pattern. Clone the configured master template and adjust a few fields rather than reconstructing the whole thing from scratch each time. With records in C# I use a 'with' expression.
A paralegal edits the Clauses list on their cloned contract, and the master template gets modified as well. What went wrong?
Shallow copy. The clone shares the same List reference as the original. I need to deep-clone the mutable members explicitly: with { Clauses = new List<string>(master.Clauses) }.
Why does 'with' behave that way?
Records copy field values, not what the fields point to. A List field holds a reference, so both records point to the same underlying list object until I explicitly clone it.
When would you not use Prototype at all?
When construction is cheap and clear. A plain constructor or factory is more readable. Prototype only pays off when copying a pre-built instance is genuinely faster or simpler than rebuilding.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Shallow vs deep copy?
followUpsQA.a: Shallow copies field values so references are shared. Deep copy duplicates the referenced objects too.

followUpsQA.q: How do records help with Prototype?
followUpsQA.a: The 'with' expression gives non-destructive copies, though still shallow for reference members.

followUpsQA.q: How do you deep clone in C#?
followUpsQA.a: Clone nested objects manually, or serialise then deserialise for a generic deep copy.

followUpsQA.q: When is Prototype worth it?
followUpsQA.a: When construction is expensive or a configured template is the simplest starting point to copy from.

questions.q: What is the Adapter pattern and where would you use it in a .NET system?

answer.oneLiner: Adapter converts one interface into the interface the client expects, letting incompatible classes work together without modifying either. It is the standard way to wrap a third-party SDK behind your own abstraction.
keyPoints: Keeps vendor SDK types out of your domain. Swap providers by writing a new adapter and changing one DI registration.
Lives in the Infrastructure layer in Clean Architecture, implementing an Application-layer interface so vendor types never leak inward.
Enables testability: callers depend on your interface, so you substitute a fake in tests instead of mocking an awkward SDK.
An adapter must contain zero business logic. Pure translation between interfaces only.
answer.detail: A wrapper class implements your target interface and delegates internally to the adaptee. The SDK's types never cross the Infrastructure boundary. The domain stays clean and the adapter is the only file that needs to change when the vendor does.

code.note: Stripe SDK types live only inside the adapter. Swap to PayPal by writing PayPalGatewayAdapter and changing one DI registration.

answer.analogy: Vendor goes in. Your interface comes out. Domain never sees it.
redFlags: Letting third-party SDK types leak into the domain or application layer. This destroys swappability.
Putting business logic inside the adapter. It should be pure interface translation, nothing more.
Confusing Adapter (converts one interface to another) with Facade (simplifies a whole subsystem).

dialogue.text: You are integrating Stripe for payments but you do not want Stripe SDK types spreading throughout your codebase. How do you isolate it?

Adapter pattern. I define IPaymentGateway in the Application layer and write a StripeGatewayAdapter in Infrastructure that implements it. Stripe SDK types stay inside the adapter and never cross that boundary.
Why go to that trouble instead of calling Stripe directly from the application?
If Stripe types spread into the domain, switching to Adyen means touching every caller. With the adapter, I write an AdyenGatewayAdapter and change one DI registration. Nothing else moves.
How does this help with testing?
Callers inject IPaymentGateway, so in tests I replace it with a fake implementation. I never have to mock the Stripe SDK, which requires a live API key and is painful to set up.
What do you absolutely not put inside the adapter?
Business logic. If I am making domain decisions inside the adapter, I have drawn the boundary in the wrong place. It translates calls. Domain rules live in the domain.
How is this different from a Facade?

Adapter converts one interface into another that the client expects. Facade simplifies a whole subsystem behind one entry point. Different intent: Adapter is about conversion, Facade is about simplification.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Adapter vs Facade?
followUpsQA.a: Adapter converts one interface to another. Facade simplifies a complex subsystem.

followUpsQA.q: Where does the adapter live in Clean Architecture?
followUpsQA.a: In Infrastructure, implementing an Application-layer interface so vendor types stay out of the domain.

followUpsQA.q: How does Adapter help testing?
followUpsQA.a: Callers depend on your interface, so you substitute a fake instead of mocking an awkward SDK.

followUpsQA.q: What must an adapter never contain?
followUpsQA.a: Business logic. Pure translation between interfaces only.

questions.q: What is the Facade pattern and how does it help manage complexity?

answer.oneLiner: Facade provides one simplified entry point over a complex subsystem so that callers make a single method call instead of orchestrating multiple services themselves.
keyPoints: Facade delegates, it does not decide. The moment it accumulates business rules it becomes a god class.
It does not change the subsystem's interfaces. It adds a simpler layer on top.
Controllers injecting four or more services and orchestrating them directly is the symptom that a Facade is missing.
Application-layer use-case handlers in Clean Architecture naturally act as Facades over domain services and infrastructure.
answer.detail: A Facade orchestrates and delegates. It should hold no business rules. Real logic lives in the domain or the individual services. Its job is to reduce coupling by giving clients one thing to depend on instead of every subsystem class.

code.note: The controller calls PlaceOrderAsync once. The Facade coordinates inventory, payment, shipping, and notifications.

answer.analogy: It delegates or it becomes a god class. No middle ground.
redFlags: Facade accumulating business logic and becoming a god object. It should coordinate, not decide.
Exposing subsystem internals through the Facade, which defeats the purpose of having one.
Confusing Facade (simplifies a subsystem) with Adapter (converts one interface to another).

dialogue.text: Placing an order touches inventory, payment, shipping, and notifications. Every controller action that creates an order duplicates the same four service calls. How do you clean that up?
Facade. A CheckoutFacade with one PlaceOrderAsync method orchestrates all four services. The controller makes a single call and is completely unaware of the steps involved.
How do you stop that Facade from turning into a god class over time?
Keep it as pure orchestration. It delegates to the services and holds no business rules. If I find myself writing conditional logic in the Facade, that logic belongs in the domain, not here.
How is a Facade different from a plain service class?
It is deliberately thin. It coordinates, it does not decide. A service class might contain domain logic. A Facade never should.
Is this not just an Adapter?

Different intent. Adapter converts one interface into another the client expects. Facade simplifies a whole subsystem behind one convenient entry point. Same structural idea of wrapping, different problems they solve.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Facade vs Adapter?
followUpsQA.a: Facade simplifies a subsystem. Adapter converts one interface into another.

followUpsQA.q: How does a Facade reduce coupling?
followUpsQA.a: Clients depend on one high-level interface, not on every individual subsystem class.

followUpsQA.q: How does it relate to use-case handlers?
followUpsQA.a: Application-layer handlers naturally act as Facades over domain and infrastructure services.

followUpsQA.q: What turns a Facade into an anti-pattern?
followUpsQA.a: Accumulating business logic instead of delegating. It becomes a god object.

questions.q: What is the Decorator pattern, and why prefer it over inheritance for adding behaviour?

answer.oneLiner: Decorator wraps an object with the same interface as the original to add behaviour at runtime, composing features by stacking wrappers instead of exploding into subclass hierarchies.
keyPoints: Both decorator and the wrapped target implement the same interface, so wrapping is completely transparent to callers.
Beats inheritance: you compose behaviour at runtime and avoid a separate subclass for every feature combination.
Order matters. Logging placed outside a caching decorator logs every call including cache hits. Logging inside only logs actual database calls.
MediatR pipeline behaviours are Decorators. Scrutor's .Decorate<T>() wires them automatically in the DI container.
answer.detail: The decorator holds a reference to the inner object, delegates the actual call to it, and adds behaviour before or after. Cross-cutting concerns such as caching, logging, and retry are the textbook use case: wrap the real service without ever touching it.

code.note: Decorator implements the same interface, delegates to the inner instance, and adds caching transparently to every caller.

answer.analogy: Stack behaviour at runtime. Never inherit it.
redFlags: Editing the original class for every cross-cutting concern. This violates the Open/Closed Principle.
Creating a subclass for every feature combination. Class explosion is the symptom Decorator cures.
Decorator that breaks the wrapped interface's contract, which is a Liskov Substitution Principle violation.

dialogue.text: You have an IProductRepository backed by EF Core. You now need to add caching, and later logging and retry, without touching the original class. How do you approach this?
Decorators. A CachingProductRepo implements IProductRepository and wraps the real EF one. A LoggingProductRepo wraps that. Each adds one behaviour and delegates everything else inward.
Why not subclass the EfProductRepository for each combination?
Class explosion. Caching plus logging, caching plus retry, all three -- a new subclass per combination, all fixed at compile time. Decorators compose at runtime, so I stack exactly the layers I need.
How do you actually wire this in .NET without manually nesting constructors?

Scrutor's Decorate extension. Register the real EfProductRepository first, then call Decorate<IProductRepository, CachingProductRepo>(). Scrutor rewires the DI graph so the decorator receives the original as its inner dependency.
Does the order of decoration matter?

Very much so. Logging placed outside the caching decorator logs every call including cache hits. Logging inside the caching decorator only logs calls that actually reach the database. Right order depends on what you want to observe.
You mentioned MediatR pipeline behaviours. How are those Decorators?

Each IPipelineBehavior<TRequest, TResponse> wraps the next step in the chain. The outermost behaviour runs first, calls next() to pass control to the next behaviour, and so on until the actual handler runs. Structurally identical to our decorator chain.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Why is Decorator better than inheritance here?
followUpsQA.a: Composes behaviour at runtime and avoids a new subclass per feature combination.

followUpsQA.q: How do you wire decorators in .NET DI?
followUpsQA.a: Scrutor's .Decorate<TInterface, TDecorator>() or manual wrapping in the composition root.

followUpsQA.q: Why does wrapping order matter?
followUpsQA.a: Each layer sees the result of the inner one. Logging outside caching also logs cache hits.

followUpsQA.q: How do MediatR pipeline behaviours relate to Decorator?
followUpsQA.a: They are Decorators over the request pipeline, structurally identical.

questions.q: What is the Proxy pattern and what kinds of proxies are there?

answer.oneLiner: Proxy is a stand-in that shares the real object's interface and controls access to it, adding lazy initialisation, permission checks, or transparent caching without the caller knowing.
keyPoints: Decorator adds behaviour. Proxy controls access and often decides whether to call the real object at all.
Four types: virtual (lazy initialisation), protection (access control), remote (network call), caching.
EF Core lazy-loading proxies are the canonical .NET example and the root cause of N+1 query problems.
Castle DynamicProxy generates proxies at runtime for AOP interception, used by some DI frameworks.
answer.detail: The proxy holds or lazily creates the real object. When a method is called, the proxy decides whether and when to forward. EF Core overrides entity classes at runtime so that accessing a navigation property transparently triggers a database query.

code.note: Proxy shares the same interface, checks permission (protection proxy) and lazily creates the real service (virtual proxy). Caller is aware of neither.

answer.analogy: Decorator adds. Proxy gatekeeps. Different jobs, same disguise.
redFlags: Confusing Proxy (controls access) with Decorator (adds behaviour). They solve different problems.
Not knowing EF Core lazy-loading proxies cause N+1. Every navigation access in a loop fires a query.
Hiding expensive work behind a property so callers do not realise they are triggering a database call.

dialogue.text: You have an expensive report generation service. You want to defer creating it until first use and also enforce a permission check before every call. How do you structure that?

Proxy implementing the same IReportService interface. It checks the permission first, then lazily creates the real service on the first call. Callers are unaware of both the permission check and the deferred creation.
How is that different from a Decorator?
A Decorator always calls the inner object and adds behaviour around it. A Proxy controls access and can decide not to call the real object at all, for example when the permission check fails.
Where does .NET use proxies in the wild?

EF Core lazy-loading proxies are the most common. When you access a navigation property on an entity, EF has replaced the entity with a proxy subclass that fires a database query transparently. The caller has no idea.
What is the danger with those EF lazy-loading proxies?

N+1 queries. If I iterate over a list of orders and access the Customer navigation property on each one, I silently fire one query per order. I would use eager loading with Include where I know I need the related data.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Proxy vs Decorator?
followUpsQA.a: Proxy controls access to the real object. Decorator adds behaviour around it.

followUpsQA.q: Name the four proxy types.
followUpsQA.a: Virtual (lazy init), protection (access control), remote (network call), caching.

followUpsQA.q: Where does .NET use proxies?
followUpsQA.a: EF Core lazy loading, gRPC and HTTP typed clients, Castle DynamicProxy for AOP interception.

followUpsQA.q: What is the N+1 risk with EF lazy-loading proxies?
followUpsQA.a: Accessing a navigation property inside a loop fires one database query per iteration.

questions.q: What is the Strategy pattern, and how does it replace a big if/switch?

answer.oneLiner: Strategy defines a family of interchangeable algorithms behind one interface and lets you select the right one at runtime, which is the canonical way to eliminate a switch statement that grows with every new variant.
keyPoints: Open/Closed Principle: adding a new variant means adding a new class and one DI registration. Zero existing code changes.
Selection is data-driven, not a switch: keyed DI services in .NET 8 or a dictionary mapping the key to the strategy.
Strategy vs State: same structure, different intent. Strategy swaps algorithms by external choice. State changes behaviour automatically as the object's own internal state changes.
Trade-off: more classes. Two simple, stable branches do not justify the ceremony.
answer.detail: Each algorithm variant is its own class implementing one interface. The context delegates to whichever strategy is currently selected. In .NET, register strategies by key and resolve at runtime so the switch statement never appears.

code.note: Adding a new tier is one new class and one AddKeyedScoped line. Zero existing code changes.

answer.analogy: A new pricing tier is a new class. The switch dies here.
redFlags: A switch or if/else chain that grows every time a new variant is added. That is Strategy waiting to happen.
Putting all variant logic in one class with branches. The switch is just hidden.
Over-engineering two simple, stable branches into three new classes.

dialogue.text: Your e-commerce platform has a pricing engine with a switch on customer tier: standard, VIP, and wholesale. Every time sales adds a new tier, a developer has to edit that switch. How do you fix this?
Strategy pattern. An IDiscountStrategy interface with one implementation per tier. The switch disappears and each tier's logic is isolated in its own class, independently testable.
How does the right strategy get selected at runtime without a switch?
Keyed services in .NET 8. Register each strategy under its tier name. At runtime, resolve using GetRequiredKeyedService with the tier string. Selection is data-driven, not code-driven.
What is the Open/Closed benefit here?
When the enterprise tier arrives, I add one new class and one registration line. No existing code is touched. I cannot accidentally break the VIP calculation while adding enterprise pricing.
Strategy and State pattern look structurally identical. What is the actual difference?

Same structure, different intent. Strategy is externally selected: the caller chooses which algorithm to use. State changes behaviour automatically as the object's own internal state changes. With Strategy I pick the algorithm. With State the object picks it for me.
When would you not use Strategy?
Two simple, stable branches that will never grow. A plain if/else is more readable than three new classes and a keyed DI registration for a permanent binary choice.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: How do you select a strategy without a switch?
followUpsQA.a: Keyed DI services in .NET 8 or a dictionary mapping the variant key to the strategy.

followUpsQA.q: How does Strategy support Open/Closed?
followUpsQA.a: A new variant is a new class and one registration. Zero changes to existing code.

followUpsQA.q: Strategy vs State?
followUpsQA.a: Strategy: external caller selects the algorithm. State: the object changes its own behaviour as internal state changes.

followUpsQA.q: When is Strategy overkill?
followUpsQA.a: Two simple, stable branches that will never grow. A switch is clearer than three new classes.

questions.q: What is the Observer pattern, and how do C# events and IObservable implement it?

answer.oneLiner: Observer lets a subject automatically notify any number of subscribers when its state changes, without coupling them directly. C# implements this natively via events and, for push streams, via IObservable<T>.
keyPoints: C# events are the built-in Observer implementation: a multicast delegate. Subscribers add handlers with +=, remove with -=.
Lapsed-listener memory leak: if a subscriber never unsubscribes with -=, the event delegate holds a reference to it indefinitely.
MediatR INotification is Observer at the application layer: publish once, fan out to every registered handler.
An unhandled exception in one subscriber aborts the remaining subscribers in the same invocation unless you isolate each call.
answer.detail: The subject holds a multicast delegate and invokes all subscribers when state changes. IObservable<T> with Reactive Extensions extends this to async push streams with composition operators, completion signals, and error channels.

code.note: Publisher raises the event once. All three subscribers are notified without the OrderService knowing who they are.

answer.analogy: Publisher never knows who is listening. That is the point.
redFlags: Forgetting to unsubscribe with -=. The event delegate keeps the subscriber alive as long as the publisher lives.
Relying on a specific order of subscriber invocation. Multicast delegates do not guarantee order.
One subscriber throwing an unhandled exception and silently killing every subsequent subscriber.

dialogue.text: When an order is placed, the email service, the inventory service, the analytics service, and the loyalty points service all need to react. How do you wire them together without coupling OrderService to any of them?
Observer pattern. OrderService raises an OrderPlaced event. Each service subscribes independently. The order service never references any of them directly.
What is the classic bug with this approach?

Lapsed-listener memory leak. If a subscriber is disposed but never calls -=, the event delegate keeps a reference to it. The garbage collector cannot reclaim it even though nothing else holds a reference.
What happens if the inventory service throws an unhandled exception?

By default the exception aborts the multicast invocation. The analytics service and loyalty service never run. I would wrap each subscriber call in a try/catch, or use MediatR's notification pattern which can isolate handler exceptions.
How does MediatR INotification compare to a C# event?

Same Observer intent but at the application layer. IMediator.Publish routes the notification to every registered INotificationHandler. Handlers are decoupled, resolved from DI, and can be added without touching the publisher.
When would you use IObservable over a plain event?

When I need stream composition: debounce, throttle, merge, or completion signals. For example, buffering inventory updates for 200ms before processing them in a batch, or merging two event streams. Plain events cannot express those shapes.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: How do C# events implement Observer?
followUpsQA.a: An event is a multicast delegate. Subscribers add with +=, subject invokes all of them.

followUpsQA.q: When use IObservable/Rx over events?
followUpsQA.a: For async push streams needing composition such as throttle, merge, debounce, or completion signals.

followUpsQA.q: What is the lapsed-listener problem?
followUpsQA.a: Forgetting -= keeps the subscriber alive through the event reference, causing a memory leak.

followUpsQA.q: How does MediatR INotification fit Observer?
followUpsQA.a: Publish once, fan out to every registered handler. Observer at the application layer.

questions.q: What is the Mediator pattern and how does MediatR apply it in a .NET application?

answer.oneLiner: Mediator centralises communication so that components talk through a hub instead of directly to each other, reducing many-to-many coupling to many-to-one. MediatR implements this by routing request objects to their single registered handler.
keyPoints: MediatR is not CQRS. It facilitates the routing. CQRS is the architectural decision to separate read and write models.
IRequest routes to exactly one IRequestHandler and returns a result. INotification fans out to many handlers with no return value.
Pipeline behaviours are Decorators over the request pipeline: validation, logging, and transactions wrap every request centrally.
Downside: navigation is indirect. You cannot jump from a request class to its handler with a single keypress.
answer.detail: The controller sends a request object through IMediator. MediatR resolves the registered handler and invokes it. Sender and handler never reference each other. The pipeline behaviour chain is itself a Chain of Responsibility.

code.note: Sender and handler never reference each other. MediatR is the hub. Pipeline behaviours add cross-cutting concerns centrally.

answer.analogy: Sender sends. Handler handles. Neither knows the other exists.
redFlags: Saying MediatR is CQRS. It is a routing tool that makes CQRS convenient, not the pattern itself.
Using MediatR in a small application with a few endpoints and no pipeline. A service call is simpler.
Handlers calling other handlers through IMediator. You recreate spaghetti through the hub.

dialogue.text: Your API controllers each inject five or six services and orchestrate them directly. Navigation is hard, testing is harder. How do you simplify the architecture?
Mediator via MediatR. Controllers send request objects through IMediator. Dedicated handlers do the work. The controller knows nothing about what happens next.
Beyond decoupling controllers from handlers, what is the practical win?

Pipeline behaviours. Validation, logging, and transaction management wrap every request centrally via IPipelineBehavior. Handlers stay focused on business logic and I never repeat cross-cutting code across endpoints.
Is MediatR the same as implementing CQRS?
No. MediatR routes messages. CQRS is the decision to have separate models for reads and writes. You can use MediatR without CQRS and you can implement CQRS without MediatR. They are independent.
What downside would you warn the team about before adopting it?

Indirection. You cannot navigate from a request class to its handler in a single keypress. For a small application with a handful of endpoints it is also unnecessary ceremony. I adopt it only when the pipeline pays off.
A developer writes a handler that calls IMediator.Send to invoke a different handler. Is that acceptable?
No. That recreates spaghetti through the mediator. If two handlers share logic, that logic belongs in a shared domain service that both depend on directly, not a mediator hop.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Is MediatR the same as CQRS?
followUpsQA.a: No. MediatR routes requests. CQRS is the read/write separation pattern. They are independent.

followUpsQA.q: IRequest vs INotification?
followUpsQA.a: IRequest goes to one handler and returns a result. INotification fans out to many with no return.

followUpsQA.q: How do pipeline behaviours relate to Decorator?
followUpsQA.a: Each behaviour decorates the request pipeline by wrapping the next step.

followUpsQA.q: When is MediatR overkill?
followUpsQA.a: A small application with a few endpoints and no cross-cutting concerns. Inject services directly.

questions.q: What is the Command pattern, and where does it show up in .NET?

answer.oneLiner: Command encapsulates a request as a first-class object, letting you queue it, log it, retry it, or undo it independently of who ultimately executes it.
keyPoints: The key insight: when a request is an object, you can store it in a database, put it on a queue, replay it later, or reverse it.
Undo/redo is a stack of command objects that each carry enough state to reverse themselves.
MediatR commands and Hangfire or Azure Queue job payloads are everyday Command pattern examples.
Command vs Strategy: Command encapsulates a specific request and its data. Strategy encapsulates a swappable algorithm.
answer.detail: The command object holds the action and its data. The invoker stores or triggers it without knowing the receiver. The receiver executes. Separating these three roles enables auditing, queuing, and undo stacks.

code.note: Command object carries the action and data. Invoker drives execution. Stack enables undo. The cart never knows about undo.

answer.analogy: A request you can save is a request you can undo.
redFlags: Wrapping trivial one-line actions in command classes when there is no queue, log, or undo requirement.
Commands that cannot undo or replay when those were the entire reason for choosing the pattern.
Confusing Command (encapsulate a specific request with data) with Strategy (encapsulate a swappable algorithm).

dialogue.text: You are building a shopping cart with an undo feature. Users can add items, apply coupon codes, and change quantities, and they expect to be able to undo each action. How do you structure this?
Command pattern. Each cart action is an object with Execute and Undo methods. I push executed commands onto a history stack. Undo pops the last command and calls Undo on it.
Why does encapsulating the action as an object specifically enable undo?

The command object carries its own data and knows how to reverse itself. AddItemCommand holds the item reference and removes it on Undo. Without this encapsulation I would need to capture and store state somewhere external, which is fragile.
Where does this same idea appear in a backend system?

Hangfire and Azure Queue job payloads are serialised command objects queued for later execution. MediatR commands are the same concept in memory. In both cases the request is an object that travels independently of its executor.
How is Command different from Strategy? Both have a single interface and one Execute method.

Different intent. Command encapsulates a specific request with its data so you can store, queue, or undo it. Strategy encapsulates a swappable algorithm. Command is about what you are doing. Strategy is about how you do it.
If you serialise a command to a message queue, what constraint does that place on the handler?

Idempotency. Message queues deliver at least once, so the handler may receive the same command more than once. Each execution must be safe to repeat without double-charging or double-booking. I include a command ID and deduplicate on the handler side.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: What does encapsulating a request as an object enable?
followUpsQA.a: Queuing, logging, retrying, and undo/redo of operations.

followUpsQA.q: Where does .NET use Command?
followUpsQA.a: MediatR commands, WPF ICommand in MVVM, Hangfire and Azure Queue job payloads.

followUpsQA.q: Command vs Strategy?
followUpsQA.a: Command encapsulates a specific request with data. Strategy encapsulates a swappable algorithm.

followUpsQA.q: How does Command support undo?
followUpsQA.a: Each command stores enough state to reverse itself via an Undo method, kept on a history stack.

questions.q: What is the Template Method pattern, and how does it differ from Strategy?

answer.oneLiner: Template Method defines an algorithm's fixed skeleton in a base class and lets subclasses override the variable steps without changing the sequence.
keyPoints: The template method itself must not be overridable. Making it sealed locks the sequence, which is the value of the pattern.
Virtual hook methods provide optional overridable steps with a shared default for subclasses that do not need to customise them.
Template Method uses inheritance and fixes variation at compile time. Strategy uses composition and fixes variation at runtime via injection.
Prefer Strategy in modern .NET. Composition is more flexible, testable, and avoids rigid inheritance hierarchies.
answer.detail: The base class calls abstract or virtual step methods in a fixed order. Subclasses fill in the variable steps. Strategy achieves the same variance by injecting the variable behaviour as a dependency, requiring no inheritance at all.

code.note: Export() locks the sequence. Subclasses fill in Filter/Format/Write. Validate is an overridable hook with a shared default.

answer.analogy: The sequence is locked. Only the steps are yours to change.
redFlags: Subclasses overriding the template method itself. This breaks the locked skeleton that is the purpose of the pattern.
Using Template Method when the steps need to vary independently at runtime. Use Strategy instead.
Deep inheritance hierarchies just to vary one step. Composition is almost always the cleaner option.

dialogue.text: Your reporting system exports data to CSV, Excel, and PDF. The steps are always filter, format, validate, and write, but each format implements filter and format differently. How do you structure this?

Template Method. A base ReportExporter has a sealed Export method locking the filter-format-validate-write sequence. CsvExporter, ExcelExporter, and PdfExporter each override Filter and Format. Validate has a shared default as a hook.
What is a hook method exactly?

A virtual method with a default implementation in the base class. Subclasses override it only when their format needs different behaviour. Validate is a good example: most formats use the same basic checks, but a PDF exporter might add digital signature verification.
Why must Export be sealed?
The locked sequence is the entire value of the pattern. If a subclass can change the order, the guarantee disappears. Sealed prevents that.
When would you switch to Strategy instead?

When I need to swap a formatting step at runtime, or compose steps independently without inheriting a whole class. Strategy injects the behaviour so I can mix and match without building a class hierarchy.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Template Method vs Strategy?
followUpsQA.a: Template Method varies steps via inheritance at compile time. Strategy swaps via composition at runtime.

followUpsQA.q: What is a hook method?
followUpsQA.a: An optional overridable step with a shared default. Subclasses extend it selectively.

followUpsQA.q: Why prefer Strategy over Template Method today?
followUpsQA.a: Composition is more flexible and testable. Inheritance hierarchies are rigid.

followUpsQA.q: What stays in the base class?
followUpsQA.a: The fixed algorithm skeleton and any steps shared across all subclasses.

questions.q: What is the Chain of Responsibility pattern, and where does ASP.NET Core use it?

answer.oneLiner: Chain of Responsibility passes a request along a sequence of handlers, each deciding to process it or pass it to the next, without the sender knowing which handler ultimately acts.
keyPoints: ASP.NET Core middleware is a direct implementation of this pattern. Each component calls next() or short-circuits by returning without calling it.
Short-circuit: a handler sets the response and returns without calling next(). Remaining middleware never runs.
Ordering is critical. Authentication must run before rate-limiting. Rate-limiting must run before routing. Wrong order can expose unauthenticated requests to the wrong handler.
MediatR pipeline behaviours and DelegatingHandler chains for HttpClient are both Chain of Responsibility.
answer.detail: Each handler holds a reference to the next. When invoked, it does its work and either terminates (short-circuits) or calls the next handler. The sender knows only the first link. Who ultimately responds is transparent.

code.note: Each link: short-circuit by returning, or pass by calling next(). Registration order determines execution order.

answer.analogy: Call next or stop. Nothing in between.
redFlags: Requests silently falling through the entire chain with no handler processing them and no response written.
Middleware registered in the wrong order. Authentication after authorisation is a common and dangerous mistake.
One monolithic handler doing everything instead of each link being focused on a single concern.

dialogue.text: Every API request needs to pass through authentication, then rate-limiting, then request logging, and any step may reject the request. How do you structure that pipeline?
Chain of Responsibility. Each concern is one link. It either rejects and stops, or passes to the next. In ASP.NET Core that is the middleware pipeline with next().
How does a link stop a request?
Short-circuit. Write the response, for example a 401 for failed authentication, and return without calling next(). All subsequent middleware is never reached.
Why does the order of middleware registration matter?

Because each handler processes sequentially. If I register rate-limiting before authentication, I count unauthenticated requests against a quota before rejecting them. An attacker could exhaust legitimate users' rate limits. Authentication must always come first.
How are MediatR pipeline behaviours a Chain of Responsibility?

Each IPipelineBehavior wraps the next one. The outermost behaviour runs first, calls next() to pass control inward, and so on until the actual handler runs. Structurally identical to the middleware pipeline.
What happens if a request falls all the way through the chain with no middleware writing a response?

The client receives an empty 200 or a connection close with no body, which is confusing and incorrect. I always have a terminal handler or fallback middleware at the end to catch unhandled requests and return a proper 404 or 400.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: Where does ASP.NET Core use this pattern?
followUpsQA.a: The middleware pipeline. Each component handles the request or calls next().

followUpsQA.q: How does short-circuiting work?
followUpsQA.a: A handler writes the response and returns without calling next(). Remaining middleware never runs.

followUpsQA.q: How do MediatR behaviours relate?
followUpsQA.a: They form a chain around the handler, each calling next() to pass control to the inner layer.

followUpsQA.q: What is the key risk?
followUpsQA.a: Requests falling through unhandled, or handlers registered in the wrong order.

questions.q: Is Dependency Injection a design pattern, and how is it more than just a framework feature?

answer.oneLiner: Dependency Injection is a pattern where a class receives its dependencies from outside rather than creating them internally. It is the practical realisation of the Dependency Inversion Principle.
keyPoints: DI is a pattern, not a framework. Hand-wiring constructors is still DI. The container automates graph resolution, nothing more.
Three lifetimes: Transient (new instance per resolution), Scoped (one per request), Singleton (one per application lifetime).
Captive dependency: a Singleton holding a Scoped service captures it for the application lifetime, making it stale and non-thread-safe.
Service Locator is the anti-pattern: pulling from the container inside a class hides its dependencies and kills testability.
answer.detail: Dependencies are declared as constructor parameters. The composition root wires them at startup. The container automates the graph, but the underlying pattern is just constructor injection. You could do it by hand without any framework.

code.note: Constructor makes dependencies explicit and testable. Service Locator hides them. Always prefer constructor injection.

answer.analogy: Name your dependencies. Hide nothing. Test everything.
redFlags: Saying DI is 'just the .NET container'. It is a pattern you can apply with hand-wired constructors and no framework.
Using Service Locator inside classes as a substitute for proper constructor injection.
Injecting a Scoped service into a Singleton (captive dependency causing stale, non-thread-safe state).

dialogue.text: Is Dependency Injection a design pattern or just an ASP.NET Core feature?

It is a genuine design pattern. The core idea is: supply a class's dependencies from outside rather than having it create them. The container automates graph resolution, but I could hand-wire constructors in a composition root and still be doing DI with no framework at all.
What is the Dependency Inversion Principle and how does DI realise it?

High-level modules should depend on abstractions, not on concretions. DI makes this practical. OrderService declares IOrderRepository in its constructor, not EfOrderRepository. I can swap the implementation without touching the consumer.
A developer registers a Singleton service that takes a DbContext in its constructor. What goes wrong?

Captive dependency. The Singleton captures the Scoped DbContext for the application lifetime. It is reused across requests, is not thread-safe, and eventually becomes stale. I would inject IServiceScopeFactory instead and create a scope per operation.
What is the anti-pattern people reach for that looks like DI but is not?

Service Locator. Pulling from the container inside the class. It hides what the class needs from its constructor signature, makes testing require a real container, and couples the class to the DI framework itself.
When would you choose Transient vs Scoped vs Singleton?

Singleton for stateless shared services like IClock or IConfiguration. Scoped for per-request things like DbContext or the current user context that must be created fresh per request. Transient for lightweight, stateless services where sharing across a request would cause problems.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: DI vs Service Locator?
followUpsQA.a: DI passes dependencies explicitly via constructor. Service Locator hides them by resolving from the container inside the class.

followUpsQA.q: What are the three lifetimes?
followUpsQA.a: Transient per resolution, Scoped per request, Singleton once per application.

followUpsQA.q: What is a captive dependency?
followUpsQA.a: A longer-lived service holding a shorter-lived one, for example a Scoped DbContext inside a Singleton.

followUpsQA.q: How does DI relate to SOLID?
followUpsQA.a: It is the practical realisation of the Dependency Inversion Principle: depend on abstractions supplied from outside.

questions.q: What is the Options pattern in .NET, and why use it over reading IConfiguration directly?

answer.oneLiner: The Options pattern binds configuration sections to strongly typed, validated classes injected via IOptions<T>, replacing raw string key lookups with type-safe objects that fail fast at startup.
keyPoints: ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart() causes the application to refuse to start on invalid config, not fail mid-request.
Three interfaces: IOptions<T> (singleton snapshot), IOptionsSnapshot<T> (per-request fresh value), IOptionsMonitor<T> (live change notifications for background services).
Consumers depend only on the settings class they need, not on the entire IConfiguration. This is Interface Segregation in practice.
Injecting IConfiguration with magic string keys is the anti-pattern: stringly typed, typo-prone, and validated only at the call site if at all.
answer.detail: services.AddOptions<T>().Bind(section) sets up binding at startup. IOptions<T>.Value returns the bound object. IOptionsSnapshot re-binds per request scope. IOptionsMonitor pushes change notifications as config reloads at runtime.

code.note: Config is bound to a typed class, validated at startup, and injected. No raw string lookups reach any consumer.

answer.analogy: Invalid config kills a startup, not a customer's request.
redFlags: Injecting IConfiguration everywhere and reading config with string keys. Stringly typed and completely unchecked.
Not calling ValidateOnStart. A bad value fails deep inside a customer request instead of at startup.
Using IOptions<T> when you need live reload. Use IOptionsSnapshot per-request or IOptionsMonitor for background services.

dialogue.text: Across the application, classes are reading configuration with lines like _config["Email:SmtpHost"]. What is wrong with this approach?

It is stringly typed and completely unchecked. A typo in the key compiles fine but returns null at runtime. A missing or misformatted value fails deep inside a request when the email is first attempted, not when the application starts.
Walk me through how you would fix this with the Options pattern.

I create a typed EmailOptions class with SmtpHost and Port as properties. Then I call AddOptions<EmailOptions>().Bind(config.GetSection("Email")).ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart(). Consumers inject IOptions<EmailOptions> and access the typed object.
How do you ensure a missing SMTP host fails at startup rather than at the point of the first email send?
Annotate SmtpHost with [Required] and call ValidateOnStart(). The application refuses to start if validation fails. The problem surfaces during deployment, not during a customer interaction.
What if the SMTP configuration changes at runtime without restarting the application?

Switch from IOptions which is a singleton snapshot to IOptionsSnapshot for a fresh value per request, or IOptionsMonitor if I am in a background service and need to be notified of changes as they happen.
How does this relate to Interface Segregation?

Consumers depend on the specific settings class they need, not on the entire IConfiguration tree. EmailSender gets only SmtpHost and Port. It has no visibility into database connection strings or feature flags. Each class declares exactly the configuration it requires.
Perfect. That's exactly the depth I was looking for. ✅

followUpsQA.q: IOptions vs IOptionsSnapshot vs IOptionsMonitor?
followUpsQA.a: IOptions is a singleton snapshot. IOptionsSnapshot reloads per request. IOptionsMonitor pushes live changes.

followUpsQA.q: Why prefer Options over injecting IConfiguration?
followUpsQA.a: Type safety, startup validation, and consumers depend only on the settings they need (Interface Segregation).

followUpsQA.q: How do you fail fast on bad configuration?
followUpsQA.a: ValidateDataAnnotations().ValidateOnStart() so the application refuses to start on invalid values.

followUpsQA.q: How does it relate to DI/DIP?
followUpsQA.a: Consumers depend on a small typed abstraction injected via the container.

questions.q: Two users book the last available seat at the same time and both succeed - how do you prevent this double-booking?

answer.oneLiner: It's a lost-update race; add a concurrency token (RowVersion) so the second SaveChanges fails with a concurrency exception instead of silently overwriting.
keyPoints: Both reads see 1 seat left, both decrement - the second write clobbers the first (lost update).
Add a RowVersion/concurrency token; EF Core includes it in the WHERE clause on UPDATE.
First commit wins; the loser matches 0 rows and throws DbUpdateConcurrencyException - catch, reload, retry or reject.
Alternatives: a pessimistic lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE) or an atomic conditional UPDATE.
answer.detail: EF Core reads the row and keeps the original snapshot. On SaveChanges it issues UPDATE ... WHERE Id=@id AND RowVersion=@original. Whoever commits first bumps the version, so the loser's WHERE matches nothing, EF sees 0 affected rows and raises a concurrency exception - then you reload the current state and decide whether to retry or tell the user it's sold out.
redFlags: Checking 'seats > 0' in app code then updating with no guard - the classic time-of-check/time-of-use race.
Never heard of optimistic concurrency or RowVersion.
Assuming a transaction alone prevents lost updates - it doesn't without a lock or a version check.

dialogue.text: Two users book the last seat at the same moment and both succeed. What went wrong?
It's a lost-update race - both reads saw one seat, both decremented, and the second SaveChanges overwrote the first without noticing.
How do you stop it without locking the whole table?
Optimistic concurrency - add a RowVersion token. EF Core puts it in the UPDATE's WHERE clause, so the first commit wins and the second matches zero rows and throws.
What do you do when that concurrency exception fires?
Catch DbUpdateConcurrencyException, reload the current seat count, and either retry the booking or tell the user it just sold out - never a silent overwrite.

followUpsQA.q: Optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency - when each?
followUpsQA.a: Optimistic when conflicts are rare (typical web apps); pessimistic locks when contention is high and retries are costly.

followUpsQA.q: How does SQL Server implement RowVersion?
followUpsQA.a: An 8-byte rowversion column the database auto-increments on every update to that row.

followUpsQA.q: Could you solve it without a version column?
followUpsQA.a: Yes - an atomic UPDATE Seats=Seats-1 WHERE Id=@id AND Seats>0 plus a rows-affected check, or a pessimistic SELECT ... FOR UPDATE.

followUpsQA.q: What is a TOCTOU bug?
followUpsQA.a: Time-of-check to time-of-use - the state changes between when you check it and when you act on it.

code.note: The token in the WHERE clause is what turns a silent lost update into a catchable conflict.

questions.q: A client retries a payment after a timeout and the customer is charged twice - how do you make the endpoint safe to retry?

answer.oneLiner: Make the write idempotent with a client-supplied idempotency key the server stores, so a retry returns the original result instead of charging again.
keyPoints: Networks give at-least-once delivery, so retries are inevitable - writes must be idempotent.
The client sends a unique Idempotency-Key header per logical operation.
The server stores key + result; a repeat key replays the saved response and skips processing.
Persist the key in the same transaction as the charge so they commit atomically.
answer.detail: On the first request you insert the idempotency key behind a unique constraint and process the charge in one transaction. A retry hits the duplicate key, so you skip the charge and replay the stored response. The key is owned by the client (stable across retries), scoped per operation, and expires via TTL.
redFlags: Relying on the client not to retry.
Generating the key server-side per request - it must be stable across retries, so the client owns it.
Storing the key outside the charge transaction, leaving a window for a double charge.

dialogue.text: A timeout makes the client retry a payment and the customer is charged twice. How do you fix it?
Make the endpoint idempotent - the client sends an Idempotency-Key, and the server records it so a retry returns the first result instead of charging again.
Where do you store that key relative to the charge?
In the same database transaction as the charge, behind a unique constraint, so either both commit or neither does.
What happens on the duplicate request?
The insert hits the unique key, I detect the duplicate, skip the charge, and replay the saved response with the original status.

followUpsQA.q: Which HTTP methods are idempotent by spec?
followUpsQA.a: GET, PUT, and DELETE are; POST is not, which is why payment POSTs need an explicit key.

followUpsQA.q: Where should the idempotency store live?
followUpsQA.a: A durable, shared store (a DB table or Redis with persistence) that survives restarts and is seen by every instance.

followUpsQA.q: How long do you keep keys?
followUpsQA.a: Long enough to cover realistic retry windows - hours to a day - then expire them via TTL.

followUpsQA.q: How is this different from queue de-duplication?
followUpsQA.a: Same idea downstream - consumers de-dupe by message id so at-least-once delivery doesn't double-apply.

questions.q: How would you rate-limit an API to 100 requests per minute per API key across a fleet of servers?

answer.oneLiner: Keep the counter in a shared fast store like Redis, keyed per API key, using a token-bucket or sliding-window algorithm enforced at the gateway.
keyPoints: Per-server counters don't work - a client spread across N servers gets N times the limit.
Use a central atomic counter in Redis keyed by api-key + window.
Token bucket smooths bursts; sliding window gives accurate per-window counts.
Enforce at the gateway before work hits downstream services; return 429 + Retry-After.
answer.detail: Use an atomic Redis operation - INCR with EXPIRE, or a Lua script for a token bucket - so two concurrent requests can't both pass the limit. A sliding-window counter is a good accuracy/cost balance. On a Redis outage you choose fail-open (protect availability) or fail-closed (protect the backend) deliberately.
redFlags: An in-memory counter per instance - it breaks the moment you scale out.
Read-then-write without atomicity, letting the limit be exceeded under concurrency.
No 429 / Retry-After response contract for clients.

dialogue.text: Rate-limit to 100 requests a minute per API key across many servers - how?
A per-server counter won't hold, so I keep one atomic counter in Redis keyed by the API key and time window, and enforce it at the gateway.
Which algorithm, and why does it need to be atomic?
Token bucket or sliding window; atomic via INCR+EXPIRE or a Lua script, so two concurrent requests can't both slip past the limit.
What if Redis goes down?
A deliberate choice - fail-open to keep the API available, or fail-closed to protect the backend - documented, not accidental.

followUpsQA.q: Token bucket vs sliding window?
followUpsQA.a: Token bucket allows controlled bursts; sliding window gives smoother, more accurate per-window counts.

followUpsQA.q: Why enforce at the gateway?
followUpsQA.a: Reject early - before the request consumes app threads, the database, or downstream calls.

followUpsQA.q: What status code and headers?
followUpsQA.a: 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After, often plus X-RateLimit-Remaining.

followUpsQA.q: How do you keep it fair across users?
followUpsQA.a: Key the bucket per identity (API key/user/IP) rather than using one global counter.

questions.q: An order touches payment, inventory, and shipping in separate services - you can't use one ACID transaction. How do you keep it consistent?

answer.oneLiner: Use a Saga - a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions that undo earlier steps when a later one fails, accepting eventual consistency.
keyPoints: There's no distributed ACID across services; two-phase commit is fragile and blocks.
A Saga is a local transaction per service plus a compensating action to roll it back.
Choreography (events) suits simple flows; orchestration (a coordinator) suits complex ones.
The end state is eventually consistent - make every step and compensation idempotent.
answer.detail: If payment succeeds but shipping fails, you run compensations in reverse - refund, release inventory. Orchestration centralizes the workflow in one service that issues commands and reacts to replies; choreography has services react to each other's events. Steps and compensations must be idempotent because messages can repeat.
redFlags: Reaching for a distributed two-phase commit across microservices.
No compensation/rollback path when a later step fails.
Assuming strong consistency where the design is inherently eventual.

dialogue.text: An order spans payment, inventory, and shipping in different services. How do you keep it consistent without one big transaction?
A Saga: each service runs its own local transaction, and if a later step fails I run compensating actions to undo the earlier ones.
Orchestration or choreography?
Choreography via events for simple flows; an orchestrator when the workflow is complex and I want one place to see and control state.
Payment went through but shipping failed - then what?
Trigger the compensations - refund the payment and release the reserved inventory - so the system settles into a consistent cancelled state.

followUpsQA.q: Why not two-phase commit?
followUpsQA.a: It blocks resources and its coordinator is a fragile single point; it scales poorly across services.

followUpsQA.q: Why must saga steps be idempotent?
followUpsQA.a: Messages are delivered at least once, so a step may run more than once and must not double-apply.

followUpsQA.q: What is the outbox pattern's role here?
followUpsQA.a: It reliably publishes the events that drive the saga without a dual-write race.

followUpsQA.q: What's the orchestration trade-off?
followUpsQA.a: Central visibility and control, at the cost of a coordinator service to build and run.

questions.q: After saving an order you publish an 'OrderPlaced' event to a broker, but sometimes the DB commits and the event never sends. How do you make it reliable?

answer.oneLiner: It's the dual-write problem; use the Transactional Outbox - write the event to an outbox table in the same DB transaction, then a relay publishes it.
keyPoints: Writing to the DB and the broker separately can't be atomic - either can fail after the other.
Outbox: insert the event into an outbox table inside the order's transaction.
A background relay or CDC reads the outbox and publishes to the broker, marking rows sent.
Delivery is at-least-once, so consumers must de-dupe.
answer.detail: Because the order row and the outbox row commit together, you never lose an event. A poller or change-data-capture process ships outbox rows to the broker and retries until acknowledged. Since the relay can re-publish after a crash, delivery is at-least-once and consumers must be idempotent.
redFlags: Publishing to the broker inside the request and assuming it always succeeds.
Trying to wrap the database and the broker in one distributed transaction.
No relay/retry - events lost on a crash between commit and publish.

dialogue.text: The order commits but the OrderPlaced event sometimes never reaches the broker. Why?
Classic dual-write - the DB and the broker are two systems with no atomic write across them, so a crash in between loses the event.
How do you guarantee the event survives?
Transactional outbox - I insert the event into an outbox table in the same transaction as the order, so they commit or fail together.
How does it actually reach the broker?
A relay or CDC process reads unsent outbox rows, publishes them, and marks them sent, retrying until the broker acks.

followUpsQA.q: Why are consumers still idempotent?
followUpsQA.a: The relay can publish a row more than once after a crash, so delivery is at-least-once.

followUpsQA.q: Poller vs CDC for the relay?
followUpsQA.a: Polling is simple; CDC (e.g. Debezium) tails the transaction log with lower latency and load.

followUpsQA.q: What is the inbox pattern?
followUpsQA.a: The consumer side - record processed message ids to drop duplicates.

followUpsQA.q: How does this relate to sagas?
followUpsQA.a: Sagas rely on reliable events; the outbox is how each step publishes them safely.

questions.q: You run three instances of a service and a nightly job fires on each, so it runs three times. How do you ensure only one runs it?

answer.oneLiner: Coordinate with a distributed lock or lease - one instance acquires it and runs the job; the others see it's held and skip.
keyPoints: Each instance has its own scheduler, so without coordination the job fires N times.
Use a shared lock/lease: a row with a unique constraint, or a Redis lock with a TTL.
The lock must auto-expire so a crashed holder doesn't block the job forever.
Make the job idempotent anyway as a safety net.
answer.detail: A simple version is an atomic acquire - INSERT a lock row, or SET NX with an expiry in Redis; whoever wins runs, the others back off. The TTL stops a dead holder from deadlocking the job, and for long jobs you renew the lease. Don't rely on the lock alone - keep the work idempotent.
redFlags: Assuming only one instance is scheduled - it breaks when you scale out.
A lock with no expiry, so a crash blocks the job indefinitely.
Ignoring lease renewal for long-running jobs.

dialogue.text: Three instances, a nightly job runs on each, so it executes three times. Fix it?
Add coordination - a distributed lock or lease in a shared store; one instance acquires it and runs, the others see it's taken and skip.
How do you stop a crash from blocking the job forever?
Give the lock a TTL so it auto-expires, and for long jobs renew the lease while the work is still in progress.
Anything beyond the lock?
Keep the job idempotent so even a rare double-run doesn't corrupt anything - belt and braces.

followUpsQA.q: How does Redis SET NX work as a lock?
followUpsQA.a: SET key value NX PX ttl sets only if absent, with an expiry - an atomic acquire.

followUpsQA.q: What is leader election?
followUpsQA.a: Electing one instance as leader (via ZooKeeper/etcd/Consul) to own singleton work.

followUpsQA.q: Why is a DB unique constraint a lock?
followUpsQA.a: Only one INSERT of the same key can succeed; the rest fail and back off.

followUpsQA.q: Risk of a TTL that's too short?
followUpsQA.a: The lease can expire mid-job and a second runner starts - size it above the job's runtime and renew it.

questions.q: Design a URL shortener like bit.ly - how do you generate the short codes and serve redirects at scale?

answer.oneLiner: Map a short code to the long URL in a key-value store; generate codes by base62-encoding a unique id, and serve cache-backed redirects.
keyPoints: The core is a code -> long-URL lookup, and reads vastly outnumber writes.
Generate codes by base62-encoding a unique counter/id, or hash + a collision check.
Cache hot codes (Redis/CDN) and return a 301/302 redirect.
Scale reads with caching and replicas; partition by code if needed.
answer.detail: A 7-character base62 code gives about 3.5 trillion combinations. Encoding a unique auto-increment id avoids collisions entirely, whereas hashing needs a uniqueness check. Redirects are the hot path, so cache aggressively and keep the store a simple key-value lookup; push click analytics off the redirect path onto a queue.
redFlags: Modeling a heavy relational schema when a key-value lookup suffices.
Random codes with no collision handling.
Doing analytics synchronously on the redirect, slowing the hot path.

dialogue.text: Design bit.ly - how do you make the short codes?
Encode a unique id in base62 - seven characters is trillions of combinations, and because the id is unique there are no collisions to resolve.
It's extremely read-heavy. How do you serve redirects fast?
Cache hot code-to-URL pairs in Redis or a CDN and return a redirect; the database is just the source of truth behind the cache.
302 if I want every hit to come back for analytics or future changes; 301 if I want browsers and proxies to cache the redirect for speed.

followUpsQA.q: Why base62 over base16?
followUpsQA.a: Shorter codes - 62 symbols pack more values per character than 16 do.

followUpsQA.q: How do you support custom aliases?
followUpsQA.a: Let users claim a code, checked against the unique-code constraint before saving.

followUpsQA.q: How do you add expiry?
followUpsQA.a: Store a TTL/expiry per code and lazily or batch-delete expired entries.

followUpsQA.q: Where do clicks/analytics go?
followUpsQA.a: Emit an event off the hot path to a queue and aggregate asynchronously.
